question:
1. Given
\[
b_{n}=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k}^{-1}, \quad n \geq 1,
\]
prove that
\[
b_{n}=\frac{n+1}{2 n} b_{n-1}+1, \quad n \geq 2
\]
and hence, as a corollary,
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} b_{n}=2
\]
solution:
Solution. We have
\[
n!b_{n}=\sum_{k=0}^{n} k!(n-k)!=\sum_{k=1}^{n+1}(k-1)!(n-k+1)!
\]

So
\[
\begin{aligned}
2 n!b_{n} & =0!n!+\sum_{k=1}^{n}[k!(n-k)!+(k-1)!(n-k+1)!]+n!0! \\
& =2 n!+(n+1) \sum_{k=1}^{n}(k-1)!(n-k)! \\
& =2 n!+(n+1)\left[(n-1)!b_{n-1}\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

Dividing by \( 2 n! \) we get
\[
b_{n}=1+\frac{n+1}{2 n} b_{n-1}
\]
as required.
Let \( b_{n}=2+C_{n} \). Then
\[
n C_{n}=1+\frac{n+1}{2(n-1)}(n-1) C_{n-1} .
\]

We shall prove by induction that \( n C_{n} \leq 6 \) for all \( n \). This is clearly true for \( n=1,2,3\left(C_{1}=0, C_{2}=\frac{1}{2}, C_{3}=\frac{2}{3}\right) \). Assume it is true for \( n=k-1 \) where \( k \geq 4 \). Then
\[
k C_{k} \leq 1+\frac{k+1}{2(k-1)} 6 \leq 1+\frac{5}{2 \cdot 3} 6=6 .
\]
where we used the fact that \( (x+1) /(x-1)=1+2 /(x-1) \) increases as \( x \) decreases for \( x>1 \). This completes the induction.

From the inequality \( C_{n} \leq 6 / n \) and the obvious fact that \( C_{n} \geq 0 \), it follows that \( C_{n} \rightarrow 0 \), and therefore \( b_{n} \rightarrow 2 \).

Remark. One can establish lim \( b_{n}=2 \) directly since
\[
\begin{array}{c}
b_{n}=2+\frac{2}{n}+\sum_{k=2}^{n-2} \frac{1}{\binom{n}{k}} \\
\leq 2+\frac{2}{n}+\frac{n-3}{\binom{n}{2}}=2+\frac{4}{n} \cdot \frac{n-2}{n-1}
\end{array}
\]
for \( n \geq 3 \).

descriptive_long.question:
1. Given
\[
termseries = \sum_{summindex=0}^{indexvar} \binom{indexvar}{summindex}^{-1}, \quad indexvar \geq 1,
\]
prove that
\[
termseries = \frac{indexvar+1}{2\, indexvar}\, prevseries + 1, \quad indexvar \geq 2
\]
and hence, as a corollary,
\[
\lim_{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} termseries = 2
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We have
\[
indexvar!\, termseries = \sum_{summindex=0}^{indexvar} summindex!\,(indexvar-summindex)! = \sum_{summindex=1}^{indexvar+1} (summindex-1)!\,(indexvar-summindex+1)!
\]
So
\[
\begin{aligned}
2\, indexvar!\, termseries &= 0!\, indexvar! + \sum_{summindex=1}^{indexvar} [\, summindex!\,(indexvar-summindex)! + (summindex-1)!\,(indexvar-summindex+1)!\,] + indexvar!\,0! \\ &= 2\, indexvar! + (indexvar+1) \sum_{summindex=1}^{indexvar} (summindex-1)!\,(indexvar-summindex)! \\ &= 2\, indexvar! + (indexvar+1)\left[(indexvar-1)!\, prevseries\right].
\end{aligned}
\]
Dividing by \( 2\, indexvar! \) we get
\[
termseries = 1 + \frac{indexvar+1}{2\, indexvar}\, prevseries
\]
as required.
Let \( termseries = 2 + offsetseries \). Then
\[
indexvar\, offsetseries = 1 + \frac{indexvar+1}{2(indexvar-1)} (indexvar-1)\, prevoffset.
\]
We shall prove by induction that \( indexvar\, offsetseries \le 6 \) for all \( indexvar \). This is clearly true for \( indexvar = 1,2,3\left(offsetseries_{1}=0,\; offsetseries_{2}=\tfrac{1}{2},\; offsetseries_{3}=\tfrac{2}{3}\right) \). Assume it is true for \( indexvar = summindex-1 \) where \( summindex \ge 4 \). Then
\[
summindex\, offsetseries_{summindex} \le 1 + \frac{summindex+1}{2(summindex-1)}\, 6 \le 1 + \frac{5}{2\cdot 3}\, 6 = 6.
\]
where we used the fact that \( (variablex+1)/(variablex-1) = 1 + 2/(variablex-1) \) increases as \( variablex \) decreases for \( variablex>1 \). This completes the induction.

From the inequality \( offsetseries \le 6 / indexvar \) and the obvious fact that \( offsetseries \ge 0 \), it follows that \( offsetseries \to 0 \), and therefore \( termseries \to 2 \).

Remark. One can establish \( \lim termseries = 2 \) directly since
\[
\begin{array}{c}
termseries = 2 + \frac{2}{indexvar} + \sum_{summindex=2}^{indexvar-2} \frac{1}{\binom{indexvar}{summindex}} \\
\le 2 + \frac{2}{indexvar} + \frac{indexvar-3}{\binom{indexvar}{2}} = 2 + \frac{4}{indexvar} \cdot \frac{indexvar-2}{indexvar-1}
\end{array}
\]
for \( indexvar \ge 3 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
1. Given
\[
meadowlark=\sum_{grasshopper=0}^{caterpillar}\binom{caterpillar}{grasshopper}^{-1}, \quad caterpillar \geq 1,
\]
prove that
\[
meadowlark=\frac{caterpillar+1}{2 caterpillar} hummingjay+1, \quad caterpillar \geq 2
\]
and hence, as a corollary,
\[
\lim _{caterpillar \rightarrow \infty} meadowlark=2
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We have
\[
caterpillar!meadowlark=\sum_{grasshopper=0}^{caterpillar} grasshopper!(caterpillar-grasshopper)!=\sum_{grasshopper=1}^{caterpillar+1}(grasshopper-1)!(caterpillar-grasshopper+1)!
\]

So
\[
\begin{aligned}
2 caterpillar!meadowlark & =0!caterpillar!+\sum_{grasshopper=1}^{caterpillar}[grasshopper!(caterpillar-grasshopper)!+(grasshopper-1)!(caterpillar-grasshopper+1)!]+caterpillar!0! \\
& =2 caterpillar!+(caterpillar+1) \sum_{grasshopper=1}^{caterpillar}(grasshopper-1)!(caterpillar-grasshopper)! \\
& =2 caterpillar!+(caterpillar+1)\left[(caterpillar-1)! hummingjay\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

Dividing by \( 2 caterpillar! \) we get
\[
meadowlark=1+\frac{caterpillar+1}{2 caterpillar} hummingjay
\]
as required.
Let \( meadowlark=2+buttercup \). Then
\[
caterpillar buttercup=1+\frac{caterpillar+1}{2(caterpillar-1)}(caterpillar-1) dandelion .
\]

We shall prove by induction that \( caterpillar buttercup \leq 6 \) for all \( caterpillar \). This is clearly true for \( caterpillar=1,2,3\left(buttercup_{1}=0, buttercup_{2}=\frac{1}{2}, buttercup_{3}=\frac{2}{3}\right) \). Assume it is true for \( caterpillar=grasshopper-1 \) where \( grasshopper \geq 4 \). Then
\[
gras shopper C_{grasshopper} \leq 1+\frac{grasshopper+1}{2(grasshopper-1)} 6 \leq 1+\frac{5}{2 \cdot 3} 6=6 .
\]
where we used the fact that \( (dragonfly+1) /(dragonfly-1)=1+2 /(dragonfly-1) \) increases as \( dragonfly \) decreases for \( dragonfly>1 \). This completes the induction.

From the inequality \( buttercup \leq 6 / caterpillar \) and the obvious fact that \( buttercup \geq 0 \), it follows that \( buttercup \rightarrow 0 \), and therefore \( meadowlark \rightarrow 2 \).

Remark. One can establish lim meadowlark=2 directly since
\[
\begin{array}{c}
meadowlark=2+\frac{2}{caterpillar}+\sum_{grasshopper=2}^{caterpillar-2} \frac{1}{\binom{caterpillar}{grasshopper}} \\
\leq 2+\frac{2}{caterpillar}+\frac{caterpillar-3}{\binom{caterpillar}{2}}=2+\frac{4}{caterpillar} \cdot \frac{caterpillar-2}{caterpillar-1}
\end{array}
\]
for \( caterpillar \geq 3 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
1. Given
\[
staticvalue=\sum_{fixedpoint=0}^{continuum}\binom{continuum}{fixedpoint}^{-1}, \quad continuum \geq 1,
\]
prove that
\[
staticvalue=\frac{continuum+1}{2 continuum} staticformer+1, \quad continuum \geq 2
\]
and hence, as a corollary,
\[
\lim _{continuum \rightarrow \infty} staticvalue=2
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We have
\[
continuum!staticvalue=\sum_{fixedpoint=0}^{continuum} fixedpoint!(continuum-fixedpoint)!=\sum_{fixedpoint=1}^{continuum+1}(fixedpoint-1)!(continuum-fixedpoint+1)!
\]

So
\[
\begin{aligned}
2\,continuum!staticvalue & =0!\,continuum!+\sum_{fixedpoint=1}^{continuum}[fixedpoint!(continuum-fixedpoint)!+(fixedpoint-1)!(continuum-fixedpoint+1)!]+continuum!0! \\
& =2\,continuum!+(continuum+1) \sum_{fixedpoint=1}^{continuum}(fixedpoint-1)!(continuum-fixedpoint)! \\
& =2\,continuum!+(continuum+1)\left[(continuum-1)!staticformer\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

Dividing by \( 2\,continuum! \) we get
\[
staticvalue=1+\frac{continuum+1}{2 continuum} staticformer
\]
as required.
Let \( staticvalue=2+chaosvalue \). Then
\[
continuum\,chaosvalue=1+\frac{continuum+1}{2(continuum-1)}(continuum-1)\,chaosformer .
\]

We shall prove by induction that \( continuum\,chaosvalue \le 6 \) for all \( continuum \). This is clearly true for \( continuum=1,2,3\left(chaosvalue_{1}=0,\, chaosvalue_{2}=\frac{1}{2},\, chaosvalue_{3}=\frac{2}{3}\right) \). Assume it is true for \( continuum=fixedpoint-1 \) where \( fixedpoint \ge 4 \). Then
\[
fixedpoint\,chaosvalue_{fixedpoint} \le 1+\frac{fixedpoint+1}{2(fixedpoint-1)} 6 \le 1+\frac{5}{2\cdot3} 6=6 .
\]
where we used the fact that \( (certainty+1)/(certainty-1)=1+2/(certainty-1) \) increases as \( certainty \) decreases for \( certainty>1 \). This completes the induction.

From the inequality \( chaosvalue \le 6 / continuum \) and the obvious fact that \( chaosvalue \ge 0 \), it follows that \( chaosvalue \rightarrow 0 \), and therefore \( staticvalue \rightarrow 2 \).

Remark. One can establish \(\lim staticvalue=2\) directly since
\[
\begin{array}{c}
staticvalue=2+\frac{2}{continuum}+\sum_{fixedpoint=2}^{continuum-2} \frac{1}{\binom{continuum}{fixedpoint}} \\
\le 2+\frac{2}{continuum}+\frac{continuum-3}{\binom{continuum}{2}}=2+\frac{4}{continuum}\cdot\frac{continuum-2}{continuum-1}
\end{array}
\]
for \( continuum \ge 3 \).

garbled_string.question:
1. Given
\[
qzxwvtnp=\sum_{yqztlvms=0}^{lkdjcqye}\binom{lkdjcqye}{yqztlvms}^{-1}, \quad lkdjcqye \geq 1,
\]
prove that
\[
qzxwvtnp=\frac{lkdjcqye+1}{2 lkdjcqye} hjgrksla+1, \quad lkdjcqye \geq 2
\]
and hence, as a corollary,
\[
\lim _{lkdjcqye \rightarrow \infty} qzxwvtnp=2
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We have
\[
lkdjcqye!qzxwvtnp=\sum_{yqztlvms=0}^{lkdjcqye} yqztlvms!(lkdjcqye-yqztlvms)!=\sum_{yqztlvms=1}^{lkdjcqye+1}(yqztlvms-1)!(lkdjcqye-yqztlvms+1)!
\]

So
\[
\begin{aligned}
2 lkdjcqye!qzxwvtnp & =0!lkdjcqye!+\sum_{yqztlvms=1}^{lkdjcqye}[yqztlvms!(lkdjcqye-yqztlvms)!+(yqztlvms-1)!(lkdjcqye-yqztlvms+1)!]+lkdjcqye!0! \\
& =2 lkdjcqye!+(lkdjcqye+1) \sum_{yqztlvms=1}^{lkdjcqye}(yqztlvms-1)!(lkdjcqye-yqztlvms)! \\
& =2 lkdjcqye!+(lkdjcqye+1)\left[(lkdjcqye-1)!hjgrksla\right] .
\end{aligned}
\]

Dividing by \( 2 lkdjcqye! \) we get
\[
qzxwvtnp=1+\frac{lkdjcqye+1}{2 lkdjcqye} hjgrksla
\]
as required.
Let \( qzxwvtnp=2+mydpltha \). Then
\[
lkdjcqye mydpltha=1+\frac{lkdjcqye+1}{2(lkdjcqye-1)}(lkdjcqye-1) vfzsnrqe .
\]

We shall prove by induction that \( lkdjcqye mydpltha \leq 6 \) for all \( lkdjcqye \). This is clearly true for \( lkdjcqye=1,2,3\left(mydpltha=0, mydpltha=\frac{1}{2}, mydpltha=\frac{2}{3}\right) \). Assume it is true for \( lkdjcqye=yqztlvms-1 \) where \( yqztlvms \geq 4 \). Then
\[
yqztlvms mydpltha \leq 1+\frac{yqztlvms+1}{2(yqztlvms-1)} 6 \leq 1+\frac{5}{2 \cdot 3} 6=6 .
\]
where we used the fact that \( (brhcvwpo+1) /(brhcvwpo-1)=1+2 /(brhcvwpo-1) \) increases as \( brhcvwpo \) decreases for \( brhcvwpo>1 \). This completes the induction.

From the inequality \( mydpltha \leq 6 / lkdjcqye \) and the obvious fact that \( mydpltha \geq 0 \), it follows that \( mydpltha \rightarrow 0 \), and therefore \( qzxwvtnp \rightarrow 2 \).

Remark. One can establish lim \( qzxwvtnp=2 \) directly since
\[
\begin{array}{c}
qzxwvtnp=2+\frac{2}{lkdjcqye}+\sum_{yqztlvms=2}^{lkdjcqye-2} \frac{1}{\binom{lkdjcqye}{yqztlvms}} \\
\leq 2+\frac{2}{lkdjcqye}+\frac{lkdjcqye-3}{\binom{lkdjcqye}{2}}=2+\frac{4}{lkdjcqye} \cdot \frac{lkdjcqye-2}{lkdjcqye-1}
\end{array}
\]
for \( lkdjcqye \geq 3 \).

kernel_variant.question:
For every integer n \geq  1 set

a_n = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{2}{\binom{n}{k}}.

(a)  Prove that for every n \geq  2

a_n = 2 + \frac{n+1}{2n}\,a_{n-1}.

(b)  Prove that the sequence (a_n) converges and find its limit.
kernel_variant.solution:
Solution.

Throughout we put

S_n := \sum_{k=0}^{n} k!(n-k)!  \qquad (n\ge 0).

Observe that

n!\,a_n = 2S_n.                                                    (1)

Part (a).

Step 1  -  A second expression for S_n.
Shift the index in the definition of S_n by writing k \mapsto  k-1:

S_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n+1} (k-1)!(n-k+1)!.                            (2)

Step 2  -  Adding (1) and (2) without double counting the
end-terms.

Both series (1) and (2) contain the terms 0!\cdot n! and n!\cdot 0!.  We add
the two sums and keep each of those boundary terms only once:

2S_n = 0!\,n! + n!\,0! + \sum_{k=1}^{n} \bigl[k!(n-k)! + (k-1)!(n-k+1)!\bigr]. (3)

Since 0!=1, the first two summands equal 2n!.

Step 3  -  A factorisation inside the bracket.
For 1 \leq  k \leq  n we have

k!(n-k)! + (k-1)!(n-k+1)! = (k + n-k +1)(k-1)!(n-k)! = (n+1)(k-1)!(n-k)!. (4)

Inserting (4) into (3) gives

2S_n = 2n! + (n+1) \sum_{k=1}^{n} (k-1)!(n-k)!.                  (5)

Step 4  -  Identifying the remaining sum.
Put j = k-1 in the last sum of (5):

\sum_{k=1}^{n} (k-1)!(n-k)! = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} j!(n-1-j)! = S_{n-1}. (6)

With (1), (5) and (6) we obtain

n!\,a_n = 2n! + (n+1) S_{n-1}.                                    (7)

From (1) with n replaced by n-1 we have S_{n-1} = (n-1)!\,a_{n-1}/2.
Insert this into (7):

n!\,a_n = 2n! + \frac{n+1}{2}\,(n-1)!\,a_{n-1}.

Finally divide by n!:

a_n = 2 + \frac{n+1}{2n}\,a_{n-1}, \qquad n\ge 2.                 (8)

That proves part (a).

Part (b).

Step 1  -  Passing to the limit.
Write (8) as

a_n = 2 + c_n\,a_{n-1} \quad\text{with}\quad c_n = \frac{n+1}{2n} \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{} \frac12.

If (a_n) converges to a limit L we must have

L = 2 + \frac12\,L \;\Longrightarrow\; L = 4.                      (9)

It remains to prove convergence.

Step 2  -  A remainder recursion.
Set C_n := a_n - 4.  Subtract 4 from both sides of (8):

C_n = \frac{n+1}{2n}\,C_{n-1} + \frac{2}{n}.                      (10)

Step 3  -  Bounding C_n.
We show by induction that

0 \le C_n \le \frac{12}{n} \qquad(n\ge 1).                       (11)

Base n = 1 :  a_1 = 2/\binom{1}{0} + 2/\binom{1}{1} = 4, so C_1 = 0 and (11) holds.

Induction step.  Assume (n-1)C_{n-1} \leq  12 for some n \geq  2.  From
(10) we obtain

nC_n = \frac{n+1}{2}\,C_{n-1} + 2 \le \frac{n+1}{2}\,\frac{12}{n-1} + 2 = 6\,\frac{n+1}{n-1} + 2.

For n \geq  4 one has (n+1)/(n-1) \leq  5/3, and hence

nC_n \leq  6\cdot (5/3) + 2 = 12.

The cases n = 2,3 can be checked directly and also satisfy (11).
Thus (11) holds for every n.

Step 4  -  Convergence.
From (11) we get |C_n| \leq  12/n \to  0, hence a_n = 4 + C_n \to  4.
By (9) this is the only possible limit, so (a_n) indeed converges
and

\boxed{\displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = 4}.

This completes the solution.

core_steps: Multiply by n! so that 1/\binom{n}{k} becomes k!(n-k)!, turning the sum into a factorial sum
Combine/shift the terms in pairs to rewrite 2·n!·b_n as 2·n! + (n+1)(n−1)!·b_{n−1}
Divide by 2 n! to obtain the linear recurrence b_n = 1 + (n+1)/(2n)·b_{n−1}
Introduce C_n = b_n − 2, translate the recurrence to n·C_n = 1 + (n+1)/(2(n−1))·(n−1)·C_{n−1}
Choose a fixed constant and prove by induction that n·C_n stays below it; this forces C_n → 0, hence b_n → 2

slot1.description: The numerical constant selected as an upper bound in the induction argument

slot2.description: The treatment of the endpoint terms k = 0 and k = n in the pairing step, which produces the standalone '+1' in the recurrence
